one_line_takeaway: CausalRM factors an RLHF reward model's latent embedding into a causal component (used for reward prediction) and a non-causal component (adversarially stripped of reward-predictive signal via gradient reversal), yielding better pairwise reward accuracy, better downstream RLHF outcomes, and markedly lower sensitivity to response-length and sycophantic-prefix artifacts than Standard RM, GoalRM, and InfoRM baselines on math and dialogue RLHF tasks.
key_finding: On reward-model pairwise accuracy (Table 1), CausalRM's ID average beats the best baseline by 1.8 points on math (70.1% vs. GoalRM's 68.3%) and 1.2 points on dialogue (72.3% vs. GoalRM's 71.1%); the gap widens OOD, reaching 85.6% avg on math OOD (+2.6 over the second best) and a 1.6-point gain over GoalRM on dialogue OOD. On downstream RLHF (Table 2, math final-answer accuracy), CausalRM reaches 74.0% ID avg and 89.6% OOD avg, versus GoalRM's 72.5%/88.5% and a notably degraded InfoRM (48.0% ID avg, 49.9% OOD avg) whose collapse the authors attribute to severe reward hacking (Figure 3 shows InfoRM's predicted reward diverging sharply from gold reward during training). On dialogue (Table 3, Qwen3-Max pairwise win rate of CausalRM-trained policies against each opponent policy, averaged over Anthropic-Helpful/Harmless for ID and MT-Bench/PKU-SafeRLHF/SHP/TruthfulQA for OOD), CausalRM's average win rate is 54.8% ID / 51.3% OOD against Standard RM, 42.3% ID / 31.6% OOD against GoalRM, and 45.5% ID / 38.7% OOD against InfoRM (each accompanied by tie/lose percentages in the source table); the paper cites these figures as evidence CausalRM policies are preferred by the LLM judge relative to the SFT reference and to baseline-RM-trained policies, most clearly against Standard RM. For causal-invariance analyses: length sensitivity (Figure 5) sigma_len = 0.03 for CausalRM vs. 0.12 (Standard RM), 0.22 (GoalRM), 0.14 (InfoRM) -- an order-of-magnitude reduction in reward's dependence on response length. Sycophancy robustness (Table 4) shows CausalRM's pairwise accuracy on the hacked test drops only -1.7 points ID / -1.1 points OOD relative to training on unperturbed data, versus Standard RM's -11.4 ID / -5.8 OOD, GoalRM's -8.9 ID / -3.8 OOD, and InfoRM's -4.0 ID / -2.5 OOD -- CausalRM is the most robust of the four to the injected sycophantic-prefix artifact.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is CausalRM, a reward-modeling framework for RLHF that decomposes the SFT backbone's contextual embedding h into two latent factors via a variational encoder: a causal factor z_c and a non-causal factor z_nc. The reward head is structurally restricted to predict reward g_psi(z_c) using only z_c. An adversarial head a_omega(z_nc) is trained to predict reward from the non-causal factor, but gradients into the encoder are passed through a gradient-reversal layer (GRL, following Ganin & Lempitsky 2015) so the encoder is pushed to make z_nc uninformative about reward. A reconstruction decoder d_eta([z_c; z_nc]) -> h_hat prevents posterior collapse/degenerate factorization. The full objective (Eq. 10) jointly optimizes a pairwise preference loss on z_c, KL information-bottleneck terms on both latents, the reconstruction loss, and the adversarial minimax term. The authors frame this as the first method to explicitly disentangle reward-irrelevant (non-causal) factors from the reward-relevant latent state in RLHF reward modeling, distinguishing it from prior work (ODIN, length-regularization DPO, MMD-based regularization) that requires pre-specifying which spurious variable to control, and from InfoRM, whose variational information bottleneck the authors say they extend by adding the explicit causal/non-causal factorization plus adversarial invariance training.
limitations: The paper's own stated future work (Conclusion) is to extend CausalRM to process reward modeling and to multi-turn dialogue with dynamic confounders, implying the current method is validated only on outcome (bandit-style, single-turn) reward modeling. The Impact Statement explicitly states the paper 'does not introduce new application domains or new data collection involving human subjects' and frames the work as reducing reliance on known spurious correlates (length, phrasing) rather than as a general defense against adversarial reward gaming; it does not claim robustness to adaptive/adversarial policies deliberately searching for unknown exploits, only to the specific length and sycophancy artifacts tested. Reward hacking mitigation is measured indirectly via gold-vs-predicted reward divergence and via the two engineered spurious-attribute probes (length, sycophantic prefix) rather than via a broad audit of possible exploit types; the causal/non-causal decomposition is learned via an unsupervised VAE-style bottleneck plus adversarial GRL rather than derived from an identified causal graph, so its correctness rests on empirical regularization rather than causal identifiability guarantees.

datasets_used: OpenMathInstruct-1 (Toshniwal et al., 2024) -- 1.8M GSM8K/MATH-derived problem-solution pairs, used to build the math preference dataset
GSM8K and MATH (ID math test splits)
Algebra222, GSM-Hard, ASDiv, MAWPS, SVAMP (OOD math benchmarks)
Anthropic-RLHF-HH (Bai et al., 2022) -- helpfulness/harmlessness preference data, ID dialogue
MT-Bench, PKU-SafeRLHF, SHP, TruthfulQA (OOD dialogue benchmarks)
ShareGPT (used for SFT of the Qwen2.5-7B dialogue backbone)
quantitative_results: Reward-model pairwise accuracy (Table 1): CausalRM math ID avg 70.1% (best baseline GoalRM 68.3%); math OOD avg 85.6% (best baseline Standard RM 83.0%); dialogue ID avg 72.3% (best baseline GoalRM 71.1%); dialogue OOD avg 62.3% (best baseline GoalRM 60.7%)

Downstream RLHF math final-answer accuracy (Table 2): CausalRM ID avg 74.0%, OOD avg 89.6%, vs. GoalRM 72.5%/88.5%, Standard RM 67.8%/83.1%, InfoRM 48.0%/49.9% (InfoRM collapse attributed to reward hacking), SFT baseline 66.9%/80.0%

Downstream dialogue Qwen3-Max win rate (Table 3): CausalRM vs Standard RM 54.8% ID / 51.3% OOD avg; vs GoalRM 42.3% ID / 31.6% OOD avg; vs InfoRM 45.5% ID / 38.7% OOD avg; vs SFT 72.3% ID / 60.5% OOD avg
Length-bias standard deviation across length buckets (Figure 5, math): sigma_len = 0.03 (CausalRM) vs 0.12 (Standard RM), 0.22 (GoalRM), 0.14 (InfoRM)

Sycophantic-prefix robustness (Table 4, pairwise accuracy drop vs. unperturbed training): CausalRM -1.7 ID / -1.1 OOD; InfoRM -4.0 ID / -2.5 OOD; GoalRM -8.9 ID / -3.8 OOD; Standard RM -11.4 ID / -5.8 OOD
baselines_compared: Standard RM (Bradley-Terry pairwise loss, linear reward head on backbone embedding)
GoalRM (Nath et al., 2024 -- contrastive, Q-function-based goal-conditioned representation learning)
InfoRM (Miao et al., 2025a -- variational information-bottleneck reward model, which the paper explicitly extends)
SFT model (reference baseline for downstream RLHF gains, not a reward model itself)
